message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 is a recently discovered pathogen responsible of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19). The immunological changes associated with this infection are largely unknown.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We evaluated the peripheral blood mononuclear cells profile of 63 patients with COVID-19 at diagnosis and the presence of association with inflammatory biomarkers and 28-days mortality.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Lymphocytopenia was present in 51 of 63 (80.9%) patients. This reduction was mirrored also on CD8+ lymphocytes (128 cells/μL), natural killer cells (67 cells/μL) and natural killer T cells (31 cells/μL). Monocytes were preserved in total number but displayed a subpopulation composed mainly of cells with a reduced expression of both CD14 and HLA-DR. A direct correlation was found between serum values of IL-6 and the frequency of Th2 lymphocytes (R=0.17;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.04) but not with the monocytes count (R=0.01;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.60). Patients who died in the 28 days from admission (N=10, 15.9%), when compared to those who did not, displayed lower mean values of CD3+ (
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.028) and CD4+ cells (
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.042) and higher mean percentages of CD8+/CD38+/HLA-DR+ lymphocytes (
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.026).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The early phases of COVID-19 are characterized by lymphocytopenia, predominance of Th2 lymphocytes and less immunocompetent monocytes, which include atypical mononuclear cells.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>eTOC</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="simple">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>-At diagnosis patients with COVID-19 have lymphocytopenia</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>-Monocytes with both normal or altered scatter properties display a reduced expression of CD14 and HLA-DR in most of COVID-19 patients</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>-Patients who die in the 28 days from admission have lower values of CD3+ and CD4+ cells and higher percentages of activated CTL cells compared to those who survive</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Early phases of COVID-19 are characterized by a reduction of lymphocyte populations and the presence of atypical monocytes

reference.unstructured: Chen, G. , D. Wu , W. Guo , Y. Cao , D. Huang , H. Wang , T. Wang , X. Zhang , H. Chen , H. Yu , X. Zhang , M. Zhang , S. Wu , J. Song , T. Chen , M. Han , S. Li , X. Luo , J. Zhao , and Q. Ning . 2020. Clinical and immunologic features in severe and moderate Coronavirus Disease 2019. J. Clin. Invest.
